planting.note: Direct sow in fall in dry, slightly acidic, well-drained soil; seeds require cold stratification to break dormancy and will germinate the following spring.

directions: Direct sow in fall in dry, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade, pressing seed into the surface without covering deeply. Established plants grow erect to arching stems clothed in soft, hoary white hairs and bear open racemes of small rose-pink to lavender pea flowers from July through September. The jointed, bristly seed segments that follow cling readily to clothing and fur, dispersing naturally without any intervention. This native perennial is highly attractive to specialist native bees in the Desmodium bee guild and requires no fertilization once established in lean, dry soil.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring after last frost in dry, sandy, acidic soil in full sun; does not transplant well and requires infertile conditions to thrive.

directions: Direct sow in spring after all frost danger has passed in dry, sandy, infertile, acidic soil in full sun; avoid enriched garden soils where this species will fail to establish. The plant produces paired leaflets and small bright yellow pea flowers from summer through fall, each blossom tucked behind a distinctive pair of large leafy bracts that give the species its common name. Seeds form in jointed, sticky segments that are consumed eagerly by bobwhite quail and mourning dove, making this species valuable for longleaf pine savanna restorations and wildlife food plots. Avoid irrigation once plants are established, as excess moisture quickly leads to root rot in this dry-adapted native annual legume.

planting.note: Broadcast seed on a firm, raked seedbed at or just after last frost; no seed covering needed as light aids germination.

directions: Broadcast scarified seed on a firm, raked seedbed in spring at or just after the last frost date, pressing lightly into contact with the soil but not burying deeply. Korean lespedeza produces small trifoliate leaves and tiny pink-purple pea flowers from late summer through fall, followed by single-seeded pods that are heavily consumed by bobwhite quail, wild turkey, and mourning dove. It is more drought-tolerant and better adapted to poor, acidic, infertile soils than crimson clover or hairy vetch, making it a reliable warm-season legume for thin or degraded pastures. Turn it under before seed set if using as a nitrogen-fixing green manure, or leave standing through winter for maximum wildlife benefit.

planting.note: Broadcast seed on a firm seedbed at or just after last frost; widely available through wildlife management suppliers; consider native Lespedeza species where habitat restoration is the goal.

directions: Broadcast seed on a firm, raked seedbed at or just after the last frost date, pressing lightly into contact with the soil surface without deep burial. Japanese lespedeza produces low-growing, branching stems with small trifoliate leaves and tiny pink-purple flowers in late summer, adapting readily to poor, dry, and acidic soils where improved clovers and vetches struggle to persist. The high seed yield makes it one of the most widely used wildlife food plot legumes in the eastern US, with quail, dove, and deer all utilizing the seeds and foliage heavily. Where native habitat restoration is the priority, the true native lespedezas — including Lespedeza capitata and Lespedeza virginica — are strongly preferred over this naturalized introduction.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost when soil is thoroughly warm; best suited to Gulf Coast and tropical zones 8-11 where long frost-free summers allow full maturity.

directions: Direct sow in warm, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy soil after the last frost date when soil temperature is consistently above 70 degrees F, covering seed to about one-half inch depth. Wild bushbean establishes quickly and produces upright racemes of striking dark purple-red pea flowers from summer through fall, followed by long, narrow, cylindrical seed pods; the vigorous growth and prolific nitrogen fixation make it a valued green manure and livestock forage crop in Gulf Coast organic farming systems. It is much less cold-tolerant than hairy vetch and will not survive frost, so it must be turned under before the first fall freeze or allowed to reseed naturally in frost-free areas. Inoculate with appropriate cowpea-type Rhizobium for maximum nitrogen fixation on first plantings in new ground.
